executive_summary.body:
GlucoTrack Lite is a mobile application that allows users with diabetes to manually log glucose readings and receive generic lifestyle nudges around diet, hydration, sleep, and physical activity. Based on the published CDSCO October 2025 SaMD draft guidance, a product of this scope likely sits within the wellness carve-out, since it does not diagnose, treat, or drive clinical management. It does not calculate insulin dose, titrate therapy, interpret readings against clinical thresholds, or generate diagnostic outputs. Consequently, the Medical Devices Rules 2017 and SaMD-specific submission pathways (MD-5, MD-7, MD-9) are not expected to be engaged at the current feature scope.

The primary regulatory exposure lies under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, because glucose readings constitute sensitive personal health data of Indian users. DPDP obligations including consent capture, privacy notice, purpose limitation, grievance redressal, and breach reporting are expected to apply. If the user base crosses the Significant Data Fiduciary threshold (typically considered at scale), additional obligations such as DPIA and independent audit may be triggered.

This Draft Pack is therefore prepared as a defensive regulatory record rather than a CDSCO submission. It documents the intended use, device description, and risk classification rationale to evidence why the product is currently scoped outside CDSCO MDR. It also flags clear regulatory triggers — dose calculation, insulin titration, AI-driven clinical recommendations, or diagnostic claims — that would likely re-classify the product as a Class B or Class C SaMD and require a fresh CDSCO assessment before such features ship.

The founder is advised to treat DPDP readiness as the immediate compliance priority and to maintain a documented feature-creep boundary so that any future shift into clinical decision support is caught before launch rather than after.
executive_summary.product_class: Wellness carve-out — not currently a regulated medical device under CDSCO MDR 2017
executive_summary.pathway: No CDSCO submission pathway applies at current scope. Primary compliance pathway is DPDP Act, 2023 (consent, notice, grievance, breach SOP). CDSCO re-assessment likely required if dose calculation, titration, or clinical decision features are added.
headline_gaps: Draft a DPDP-aligned privacy notice and granular consent flow for glucose data, and appoint a grievance officer with a documented breach response SOP.
Document an internal feature-creep boundary policy that flags dose calculation, insulin titration, or AI-based clinical advice as triggers for CDSCO re-assessment before launch.
Adopt lightweight ISO 27001-style software hygiene controls (access logs, encryption-at-rest, change management) to support DPDP defensibility and future regulator readiness.

intended_use.indication: GlucoTrack Lite is intended as a general wellness and self-tracking tool for adults living with diabetes who wish to maintain a personal log of self-measured blood glucose readings and receive non-clinical lifestyle nudges related to diet, hydration, sleep, and physical activity. The application does not diagnose diabetes, does not interpret readings against clinical decision thresholds, does not recommend or calculate insulin or oral hypoglycaemic doses, and does not replace consultation with a registered medical practitioner. It is positioned as a behavioural and lifestyle support tool, not a clinical decision support system.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended user is the individual adult consumer with diagnosed diabetes who self-monitors glucose using a separate, independently regulated glucometer or CGM and chooses to keep a digital log. The application is consumer-facing and direct-to-user; it is not intended for use by clinicians as a clinical workflow tool, nor is it intended to be procured by hospitals or laboratories for diagnostic purposes.
intended_use.use_environment: The application is used in non-clinical, everyday settings such as the home, workplace, or while travelling, on the user's personal smartphone (Android or iOS). It does not require a clinical environment, calibration, or supervised use. Internet connectivity is needed for cloud sync, account management, and content delivery, but core logging may function offline.
intended_use.contraindications: GlucoTrack Lite is not intended for use as a substitute for clinical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. It is not intended for medication dosing decisions, insulin titration, hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia emergency response, gestational diabetes management decisions, paediatric diabetes management, or any acute clinical scenario. Users experiencing symptoms of acute hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia are directed in-app to contact a registered medical practitioner or emergency services.

device_description.components_architecture: GlucoTrack Lite consists of a consumer-facing mobile application (Android and iOS) and a supporting cloud backend used for user authentication, encrypted storage of logs, and delivery of lifestyle content. The architecture is a standard three-tier consumer app: mobile client, API gateway with application services, and a managed database with encryption-at-rest. There is no integration with regulated medical devices, no closed-loop control, no clinician-facing module, and no algorithmic interpretation of readings.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The user manually enters self-measured glucose readings (mg/dL or mmol/L), along with optional context such as meal, activity, or mood. The application stores these entries chronologically, renders trend charts for the user's personal review, and surfaces generic, pre-authored lifestyle nudges (e.g. hydration reminders, walking prompts, sleep hygiene tips). Nudges are rule-based on non-clinical signals such as time of day or logging frequency and are not personalised against clinical thresholds. No diagnostic, prognostic, or treatment recommendation is generated.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only consumer application, no biocompatibility or physical materials standards apply. Relevant voluntary references include IEC 62304 software lifecycle principles adopted in lightweight form, OWASP Mobile Top 10 for application security, and ISO/IEC 27001-aligned controls for data handling. DPDP Act, 2023 obligations govern personal data processing.
device_description.variants_accessories: A single application variant is offered across Android and iOS. There are no hardware accessories, no companion devices, and no integrations with regulated glucometers or CGMs at current scope. Optional ABHA linking for user-initiated record sharing may be considered in future but is not part of the current release.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Lifecycle management follows standard mobile application practice: versioned releases through Play Store and App Store, in-app update prompts, and deprecation notices for legacy versions. On account deletion, user data is purged from production systems within a documented retention window aligned to DPDP requirements, with backup expiry following the organisation's data retention SOP. No physical disposal considerations apply.

risk_classification.imdrf_significance: Not applicable — the application does not provide information used to drive, inform, or treat clinical management at current scope.
risk_classification.imdrf_situation: Not applicable — no clinical situation is addressed; the use context is general wellness and self-tracking.
risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: If the IMDRF SaMD framework were forcibly applied, GlucoTrack Lite would map to the lowest cell — informing clinical management in a non-serious situation — which corresponds to Category I. However, the more accurate position is that the framework does not engage at all, because the application neither informs nor drives clinical management. It logs user-entered values and surfaces generic lifestyle content. Under the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD draft guidance, manual logging tools and generic wellness nudges typically sit within the wellness carve-out and are not treated as SaMD. Category I is recorded here only as a conservative anchor for documentation; the operative position is wellness carve-out.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017, software qualifies as a medical device only when it has a medical purpose such as diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, treatment, or alleviation of disease. GlucoTrack Lite at current scope does none of these in a regulated sense — it is a passive log plus generic lifestyle content and does not interpret, recommend, or drive clinical action. The operative position is therefore that CDSCO MDR does not apply and no class designation is engaged. Class A is noted only as the closest conservative anchor if a regulator were to take an expansive view; in that hypothetical, the lowest risk class would likely apply, with an MD-5 pathway. The product policy commits to triggering a fresh CDSCO classification review before shipping any feature involving dose calculation, insulin titration, AI-driven clinical recommendations, or diagnostic claims, since such features would likely reclassify the product to Class B or Class C SaMD.

clinical_context.clinical_need: India has a large and growing population living with type 2 diabetes, and self-management between clinical visits is a recognised behavioural challenge. Tools that help users maintain a consistent log of self-measured glucose readings and receive lifestyle reinforcement may support adherence and engagement. GlucoTrack Lite addresses this behavioural and lifestyle support need without entering clinical decision-making territory.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Since the product is positioned within the wellness carve-out, a formal predicate device analysis under CDSCO MDR is not engaged. For market positioning, the product sits alongside general wellness and habit-tracking applications rather than alongside regulated SaMD products such as insulin dose calculators or CGM analytics platforms. If the product later adds clinical decision features, a predicate analysis against CDSCO-licensed SaMD products in the diabetes management space would likely be required.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: No clinical investigation under MDR Chapter VII is anticipated at current scope. Evidence generation focuses on usability testing, user engagement metrics, and qualitative feedback to refine the lifestyle nudge content. If the product later expands into clinical decision support, an evidence plan including clinical performance evaluation, possibly a clinical investigation under an MD-12 test licence, and post-market surveillance under MD-14 would likely become applicable and would be planned in advance of feature launch.

executive_summary.body:
GlucoTrack Lite is a consumer-facing mobile application that enables individuals with diabetes to manually log glucose readings and receive generic lifestyle nudges. Based on the product description and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, the application's current feature set — manual data entry and non-clinical behavioural prompts — likely falls within the wellness carve-out and outside the scope of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR 2017). No CDSCO classification, registration, or manufacturing licence is expected to be triggered at current scope. The primary compliance obligation is the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act), because glucose readings constitute sensitive personal health data of Indian users.

The company's near-term regulatory posture is therefore oriented around two parallel tracks. First, achieving DPDP readiness: establishing a lawful consent mechanism, publishing a DPDP-aligned privacy notice, appointing a grievance officer, and operationalising a breach-response SOP. Second, maintaining a documented feature-boundary policy that flags any future product capability — such as insulin dose calculation, insulin titration guidance, or AI-driven clinical recommendations — as a formal regulatory trigger requiring CDSCO SaMD re-assessment before that feature is launched.

Should the product roadmap evolve to include clinical decision support that drives treatment management, the classification analysis is expected to shift materially. At that stage, IMDRF SaMD category II or III would likely apply, mapping to CDSCO Class B or Class C, and an MD-7 or MD-9 application pathway would become relevant. Founders are advised to treat the present wellness period as preparation time: establishing lightweight ISO 27001-aligned software quality controls and beginning a predicate-device landscape review now will meaningfully reduce the compliance lift if and when that reclassification occurs.
executive_summary.product_class: Wellness carve-out — not classified as a medical device at current scope
executive_summary.pathway: No CDSCO submission pathway applicable at current scope. DPDP Act compliance is the operative regulatory pathway. Re-assessment against MDR 2017 and the CDSCO Oct 2025 SaMD draft is recommended before launching any dose-calculation, insulin-titration, or AI-driven clinical advisory feature.
headline_gaps: Draft and deploy a DPDP-aligned privacy notice and granular consent flow for glucose data, appoint a grievance officer, and document a breach-response SOP.

Formalise an internal product-boundary policy identifying dose calculation, insulin titration, and AI clinical recommendations as CDSCO SaMD re-assessment triggers before any such feature is launched.
Implement lightweight ISO 27001-style software hygiene controls (encryption-at-rest, access logging, change management) to support DPDP defensibility and future regulator readiness.

intended_use.indication: GlucoTrack Lite is intended to support self-management by individuals with a pre-existing diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. The application provides a structured digital interface for users to manually record glucose readings obtained from an external glucometer or laboratory test, and delivers generic, non-personalised lifestyle nudges (relating to diet, physical activity, and medication adherence reminders) based on user-entered data. The application does not interpret glucose values for diagnostic purposes, does not calculate or recommend insulin or medication doses, and does not replace clinical advice from a registered medical practitioner.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended user is an adult individual with a self-reported or clinician-diagnosed diabetes mellitus condition who is capable of independently operating a smartphone. The application is designed for direct consumer use without intermediation by a healthcare professional. It is not intended for use by minors without parental supervision, nor is it intended for use by clinical staff as a point-of-care tool.
intended_use.use_environment: The application is intended for use in non-clinical, home, or community settings on a personal Android or iOS smartphone with an active internet connection. It is not intended for use in intensive care units, emergency departments, or any setting where real-time clinical decision support is required. The use environment assumes a lay user operating the device in a low-acuity, self-monitoring context.
intended_use.contraindications: GlucoTrack Lite is not intended for individuals who rely solely on the application's lifestyle nudges as a substitute for prescribed medical treatment or professional dietary counselling. The application is not indicated for use in gestational diabetes management where clinical oversight is continuous. It is not suitable as a sole data-capture tool in clinical trials or formal observational studies. The application should not be used to make any decision regarding insulin dose adjustment or any other pharmacological intervention.

device_description.components_architecture: GlucoTrack Lite is a software-only mobile application distributed via consumer app stores (Google Play Store and Apple App Store). The system architecture comprises: (1) a client-side mobile application layer (iOS and Android) providing the user interface for data entry, nudge display, and historical log visualisation; (2) a cloud-based backend service handling user account management, data storage, and nudge-content delivery; and (3) a data persistence layer storing user-entered glucose logs and profile information. No proprietary hardware component, sensor, or physical device is bundled with the application. The application does not interface directly with any glucometer via Bluetooth, NFC, or USB at current scope.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The application operates on a manual data-entry model. Users input glucose readings — obtained independently from a personal glucometer or laboratory report — into a structured log within the app. The application timestamps and stores these entries in the user's personal health log. Lifestyle nudges are delivered based on pre-authored content rules (e.g., time-of-day reminders, general dietary guidance for diabetics) and do not involve algorithmic interpretation of the user's individual glucose trends for clinical decision purposes. The nudge engine is rule-based and non-adaptive at current scope; no machine learning or AI inference is applied to user data.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only product, GlucoTrack Lite does not incorporate physical materials. The software is developed in accordance with generally accepted secure software development lifecycle (SDLC) practices. Data transmission between the mobile client and backend is expected to use TLS 1.2 or higher encryption. Data at rest is expected to be encrypted using AES-256 or equivalent. The application is expected to be assessed against OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard (MASVS) controls. Alignment with ISO/IEC 27001 information security management principles is recommended as a baseline quality posture.
device_description.variants_accessories: At current scope, GlucoTrack Lite is offered as a single application variant available on iOS and Android platforms. No hardware accessories, companion devices, or premium clinical-feature tiers are described. If a future variant incorporates Bluetooth glucometer integration, dose-calculation functionality, or AI-driven trend analysis, that variant would be expected to require separate regulatory classification review under MDR 2017 prior to launch.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: The application lifecycle is managed through standard app-store update mechanisms. Version updates are expected to be documented through a formal change-management log. On user account deletion, personal health data — including glucose logs — is expected to be purged from backend systems in accordance with DPDP Act obligations regarding erasure of personal data on withdrawal of consent or account closure. No physical disposal procedure is applicable given the software-only nature of the product.

risk_classification.imdrf_significance: Inform clinical management (trending/informing only) — or wellness/prevention (non-clinical at current scope)
risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorisation Framework, the two axes are significance of information provided by the SaMD and the healthcare situation or condition. GlucoTrack Lite, at current scope, provides only user-entered log data and generic lifestyle nudges; it does not treat, diagnose, or drive clinical management decisions. The healthcare situation is non-critical wellness self-monitoring in a stable diabetic individual. Mapping these axes places the product at IMDRF Category I — the lowest risk tier. This assessment is contingent on the product not providing glucose interpretation, dosing guidance, or any output intended to directly influence a clinical intervention. Any evolution toward driving clinical management in a serious or critical situation would re-map to Category II, III, or IV.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, software that performs only manual data logging and delivers non-personalised wellness content is likely to be treated as a wellness carve-out, not a SaMD requiring classification. However, if the product is ever formally assessed under MDR 2017 — for example following a feature expansion — its IMDRF Category I mapping would correspond to CDSCO Class A: low individual risk, low public health risk. Class A devices are subject to the lightest regulatory scrutiny under MDR 2017. No manufacturing licence (Form MD-5) or import/domestic manufacturing licence under MD-7 or MD-9 is expected to be applicable at current scope.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Diabetes mellitus affects an estimated 101 million adults in India (ICMR-INDIAB, 2023), with a substantial proportion managing the condition in community settings with limited access to continuous clinical supervision. Structured self-monitoring of blood glucose is recognised by clinical guidelines (including ADA Standards of Care and RSSDI guidelines) as a component of diabetes self-management. A mobile application that facilitates regular glucose logging and reinforces healthy behaviours addresses a documented engagement gap in this population, particularly for patients managed on oral agents or lifestyle modification who may not have frequent clinical contact.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Several consumer glucose-logging applications have been commercially available internationally and in India, including MySugr (Roche), mySugr logbook, and Diabeto, as well as generic health diary applications. These products operate on comparable manual-entry models and have not been classified as medical devices in most jurisdictions under equivalent wellness-carve-out provisions. These applications serve as functional reference points for GlucoTrack Lite's intended use and risk profile, though they do not constitute formal CDSCO predicates in the substantial-equivalence sense applicable to Class C/D devices.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: Given the current wellness carve-out status, no formal clinical investigation or ICMR ethics review is expected to be required at current scope. Should the product later expand to include features that drive clinical management, a prospective usability study and real-world evidence plan would be recommended, including a study assessing whether the application's nudges are associated with improved glycaemic outcomes in the target population. At current scope, evidence activity is recommended to focus on: (1) usability testing with a representative sample of diabetic users aged 30–65; (2) logging accuracy verification (user interface validation); and (3) a DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) to satisfy DPDP obligations.

executive_summary.body:
CardioAI Triage is an artificial-intelligence software-as-a-medical-device (SaMD) intended to analyse 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) images and flag findings suggestive of acute myocardial infarction (MI) for clinician review. Based on the IMDRF risk framework and the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017, the product is likely classifiable as a Class C device, given that it informs clinical management decisions for a serious-to-critical cardiac state. The October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance further reinforces that AI-based clinical decision support tools operating in cardiology fall squarely within medical-device scope.

The expected pathway is a central licensing route: an MD-12 test license to support clinical investigation activities, a clinical investigation conducted under MD-22/MD-23 with prior Ethics Committee approval and CTRI registration, followed by an MD-9 manufacturing license issued by the Central Licensing Authority. Because the model is adaptive AI/ML, an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) and Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) are likely to be expected as part of the technical file.

Current readiness is low. Foundational elements typically expected by CDSCO reviewers — an ISO 13485 quality management system, an IEC 62304-compliant software lifecycle, and prospective clinical validation — are not yet evidenced. The realistic timeline to a submittable dossier is approximately 18–28 months on a clean run, with the critical path running through QMS stand-up, software lifecycle remediation, and the clinical investigation. Parallel obligations under the DPDP Act 2023 (sensitive ECG image data) and ICMR 2023 AI ethics guidelines are also likely to apply. This Draft Pack scaffolds the major dossier sections so that founders can engage a CDSCO consultant and notified body with a structured starting point rather than a blank page.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic central pathway — MD-12 test license to support clinical investigation, MD-22/MD-23 for clinical investigation under EC approval and CTRI registration, followed by MD-9 manufacturing license from the Central Licensing Authority
headline_gaps: Stand up an ISO 13485 QMS scoped to a Class C AI-SaMD; engage a consultant for gap assessment within the next 30 days.
Implement an IEC 62304 software lifecycle at Software Safety Class C, including version control, risk-traced requirements, and V&V protocols.
Plan an EC-approved retrospective validation against cardiologist-adjudicated ECGs, followed by a CTRI-registered prospective clinical investigation.

intended_use.indication: CardioAI Triage is intended as a clinical decision support tool that analyses 12-lead ECG images and flags findings suggestive of acute myocardial infarction (MI), including ST-elevation and non-ST-elevation patterns, to assist qualified clinicians in triage and prioritisation. The output is advisory and is not intended to replace clinician interpretation, definitive diagnosis, or downstream confirmatory testing such as cardiac biomarkers or coronary angiography.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are qualified medical practitioners, including emergency physicians, cardiologists, intensivists, and trained physician assistants operating under physician supervision. The tool is not intended for use by patients, untrained operators, or for self-diagnosis. User competency is expected to include ECG interpretation literacy and familiarity with institutional MI triage protocols.
intended_use.use_environment: The device is intended for use in clinical care settings such as hospital emergency departments, chest pain units, cardiac care units, and tele-cardiology hubs serving secondary and tertiary care facilities. It may also be deployed in screening clinics where ECG acquisition equipment is available. Use assumes a stable computing environment with appropriate display hardware and integration with hospital workflow systems.
intended_use.contraindications: The output is not intended for paediatric ECGs, ECGs from patients with known left bundle branch block, paced rhythms, or other confounding morphologies unless explicitly validated. The device is not intended for use as a sole determinant of clinical action and is contraindicated as a standalone diagnostic in the absence of clinician review. ECGs of poor signal quality or with significant artefact may not be reliably analysed.

device_description.components_architecture: CardioAI Triage is a software-only medical device (SaMD) comprising an image-ingestion module, a pre-processing pipeline that normalises ECG images, a deep-learning inference engine trained to detect MI-suggestive patterns, and a clinician-facing user interface that presents flagged findings with confidence indicators. The system is expected to be deployable as a cloud-hosted service or an on-premise instance, with API-based integration to hospital information systems and PACS-like image stores. No proprietary hardware is bundled.
device_description.principle_of_operation: An ECG image, captured from a standard 12-lead ECG machine printout or digital export, is uploaded to the system. The pre-processing layer normalises image orientation, lead segmentation, and signal extraction. The trained convolutional neural network model then infers the probability of MI-associated patterns and returns a flag (positive/negative/indeterminate) along with a confidence score and the leads of interest. The clinician reviews the flag alongside the original ECG and clinical context before any management decision.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, no biocompatible materials are involved. Applicable standards likely include ISO 13485:2016 (QMS), IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle, anticipated Software Safety Class C), ISO 14971 (risk management), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), ISO/IEC 27001 (information security), and emerging guidance under the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD draft. AI/ML-specific considerations align with IMDRF and ICMR 2023 AI ethics guidance.
device_description.variants_accessories: No physical accessories are bundled. Variants may include deployment configurations (cloud, on-premise, hybrid) and integration adapters for different hospital information systems or ECG device vendors. Each deployment variant is expected to be covered under a single device family in the technical file, with configuration-specific verification evidence.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Device lifecycle includes versioned software releases under change control, periodic model retraining governed by a Predetermined Change Control Plan, and end-of-support notifications to deployed sites. Decommissioning involves secure deletion of patient data in accordance with the DPDP Act 2023, revocation of API credentials, and archival of audit logs for the regulatory retention period. No physical disposal is involved.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: CardioAI Triage informs clinical management decisions in a serious-to-critical clinical situation. Acute myocardial infarction is a time-sensitive condition where triage delay or misclassification may lead to significant morbidity or mortality. The device drives clinical management by directing clinician attention to ECGs likely to represent MI, influencing prioritisation and downstream confirmatory workup. Mapping these two axes — drive clinical management × serious situation — onto the IMDRF SaMD framework places the device in Category III. While the underlying condition can escalate to critical, the device's output is advisory and reviewed by a qualified clinician before action, which is consistent with Category III rather than Category IV.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules 2017 and the risk-based classification framework adopted by CDSCO, software intended for diagnosis or to influence the management of serious diseases is typically placed in Class C. The IMDRF Category III mapping aligns with a CDSCO Class C designation: moderate-to-high risk, central licensing authority jurisdiction, MD-9 manufacturing license, and clinical investigation expectations under MD-22/MD-23. The October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance further reinforces that AI-based clinical decision support tools targeting cardiovascular conditions likely fall within Class C. A Class D call would typically apply only where the software directly drives a critical, irreversible therapeutic action without meaningful clinician interposition, which is not the case here.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Acute myocardial infarction remains a leading cause of cardiovascular mortality in India, and door-to-balloon and door-to-ECG-interpretation times are well-established determinants of outcome. In high-volume emergency departments and tele-cardiology networks, ECG interpretation backlogs and variability in non-cardiologist reads contribute to delayed recognition of MI. An AI triage layer that flags likely MI ECGs for prioritised clinician review may help reduce time-to-recognition, particularly in resource-constrained settings and rural tele-ECG workflows.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Several internationally CE-marked or US FDA-cleared AI ECG interpretation tools exist in adjacent indications, including arrhythmia detection and broader ECG triage. Direct CDSCO-licensed predicates for ECG image-based MI detection are not yet well established in the Indian market. Predicate analysis is expected to draw on international clearances as supportive context, while the CDSCO submission is likely to proceed on the basis of independent clinical evidence rather than substantial-equivalence claims.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence plan is expected to follow a two-stage approach. Stage 1: an Ethics Committee-approved retrospective validation against a cardiologist-adjudicated ECG dataset, stratified by MI subtype, demographic factors, and acquisition device, reporting sensitivity, specificity, AUROC, and subgroup performance. Stage 2: a CTRI-registered prospective clinical investigation under MD-22/MD-23 at NABH-accredited cardiac centres, comparing AI-assisted triage against standard workflow on time-to-recognition and clinical decision concordance, conducted in alignment with ICMR 2023 AI ethics guidance.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to be included in the technical file given the AI/ML nature of the device. The PCCP likely scopes the types of model updates anticipated post-market — for example, periodic retraining on expanded ECG datasets, threshold recalibration, and incorporation of new ECG acquisition device formats — together with the boundaries within which such changes may be made without triggering a fresh license amendment. Changes outside the PCCP envelope are expected to be routed through a formal CDSCO change notification.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol describes the methods used to implement and validate changes within the PCCP envelope. It is expected to cover dataset governance (provenance, bias auditing, demographic representativeness), model training and validation procedures, performance acceptance criteria (sensitivity, specificity, subgroup parity thresholds), regression testing against a locked benchmark set, and documentation requirements under the IEC 62304 lifecycle. Sign-off authorities and traceability between change requests, validation evidence, and release notes are likely to be specified.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring is expected to include ongoing tracking of model output distributions, clinician override rates, flagged-case outcome correlation where feasible, and site-level performance drift indicators. A periodic safety and performance review — likely quarterly in the first year and semi-annually thereafter — feeds into the PCCP governance loop. Material drift or safety signals trigger pre-defined response actions, including clinician advisories, model rollback, or CDSCO notification under the Medical Device Rules vigilance provisions.

executive_summary.body:
CardioAI Triage is a Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) that applies artificial intelligence to electrocardiogram (ECG) images to detect findings indicative of myocardial infarction (MI) and generate triage flags for clinical review. The product is intended for use by qualified healthcare professionals in acute and emergency care settings across India. Based on the IMDRF SaMD risk framework and India's Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR 2017), the device is assessed as IMDRF Category III and CDSCO Class C, reflecting the combination of a 'Drive clinical management' information output operating in a 'Serious' healthcare situation. This classification places CardioAI Triage within the central CDSCO licensing pathway.

The regulatory route likely involves a test license application (Form MD-12) to support clinical investigation, followed by a clinical investigation conducted under CDSCO's MD-22/MD-23 framework at CTRI-registered sites, and ultimately a domestic manufacturing license via Form MD-9 issued by the Central Licensing Authority. The October 2025 CDSCO Draft SaMD Guidance, once finalised, is expected to formalise AI-CDS product requirements including algorithm change protocols and post-market performance monitoring, and CardioAI Triage should be positioned to satisfy those provisions.

At present, the product's regulatory readiness is assessed as low across all material dimensions. No Quality Management System aligned to ISO 13485 has been evidenced, no IEC 62304-compliant software lifecycle documentation is in place, and no clinical validation evidence against an adjudicated ECG dataset has been produced. These three gaps represent the most consequential obstacles to submission. A realistic timeline to first CDSCO license, assuming no resubmissions, is approximately 18–28 months, anchored on a 6–9 month QMS stand-up, a 9–12 month clinical investigation, and concurrent technical file preparation. Founders are advised to initiate QMS scoping and an ethics committee consultation immediately to preserve timeline.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing — MD-12 test license application to CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, clinical investigation under MD-22/MD-23 framework with CTRI registration, followed by MD-9 Class C central manufacturing license
headline_gaps: Establish an ISO 13485-aligned QMS immediately: engage a consultant for a gap assessment scoped to a Class C AI-SaMD.
Stand up an IEC 62304 Software Safety Class C-compliant SDLC covering risk-traced requirements, version control, and V&V protocols.
Initiate EC-approved retrospective clinical validation against cardiologist-adjudicated ECGs as the precursor to a CTRI-registered prospective study.

intended_use.indication: CardioAI Triage is intended to analyse resting 12-lead ECG images and generate a triage flag indicating the likelihood of findings consistent with acute myocardial infarction. The output is intended to assist qualified clinicians in prioritising patient assessment and is not intended to replace physician interpretation or serve as a standalone diagnostic conclusion.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are qualified healthcare professionals — including emergency physicians, cardiologists, trained nurses, and paramedical staff operating in supervised clinical environments — who have the training to contextualise AI-generated outputs within the broader clinical presentation of the patient. The device is not intended for use by laypersons or patients without clinical supervision.
intended_use.use_environment: CardioAI Triage is intended for use in acute care and emergency settings, including hospital emergency departments, cardiac care units, and triage stations where 12-lead ECG acquisition is routinely performed. The device operates as a software application accessible via a clinical workstation, tablet, or integrated hospital information system. It is not intended for home use or unsupervised remote settings.
intended_use.contraindications: CardioAI Triage is likely contraindicated for use as the sole basis for treatment decisions, including reperfusion therapy initiation, without corroborating clinical assessment by a qualified physician. The device may not be appropriate for ECG images of suboptimal technical quality, paediatric populations, or patients with pre-existing conditions that significantly alter ECG morphology (e.g., left bundle branch block, ventricular paced rhythms), unless specific validation evidence for such subgroups is available. Use outside the validated image acquisition parameters is not recommended.

device_description.components_architecture: CardioAI Triage consists of a cloud-hosted or on-premises inference engine, an ECG image ingestion module, a pre-processing pipeline, an AI inference model, and a results delivery interface. The software architecture is expected to include a front-end clinical dashboard, a secure API layer for integration with hospital information systems or ECG acquisition hardware, and an audit logging module for traceability. The system likely interfaces with standard 12-lead ECG output formats, including raster image and PDF exports from ECG machines. All components together constitute the SaMD boundary for regulatory purposes.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The device applies a trained deep-learning model — likely a convolutional neural network or transformer-based architecture — to digitised 12-lead ECG images. The model extracts morphological and temporal features from waveform representations and maps them to a probabilistic output indicating the likelihood of ECG findings consistent with myocardial infarction. The output is presented as a triage flag (e.g., high/medium/low concern) accompanied by relevant waveform highlighting to support clinical review. The model is trained and validated on labelled ECG datasets with cardiologist-adjudicated ground truth.
device_description.materials_standards: As a pure software device, CardioAI Triage has no physical material composition. Applicable standards are expected to include IEC 62304 (software lifecycle processes), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), ISO 14971 (risk management), ISO 13485 (quality management system), and ISO/IEC 27001 (information security management). Alignment with ICMR's Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare (2023) and the CDSCO Draft SaMD Guidance (October 2025, once finalised) is additionally expected.
device_description.variants_accessories: CardioAI Triage may be offered in deployment variants, including a cloud-based SaaS model and an on-premises installation for hospitals with data residency requirements. Potential accessories include ECG image upload utilities and HL7/FHIR integration connectors for hospital EHR systems. Each deployment variant and integration module with a distinct intended use or performance boundary may require separate regulatory consideration. No hardware accessories are anticipated as part of the core device.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: As a software-only device, the lifecycle of CardioAI Triage spans initial deployment, version-controlled updates, algorithm retraining events, and eventual decommissioning. Software disposal involves secure deactivation of cloud instances, revocation of access credentials, and patient data deletion or transfer in accordance with DPDP Act obligations and applicable data retention policies. Post-market surveillance activities are expected to continue for the full deployed lifetime of each software version.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorisation Framework (N12), CardioAI Triage maps to the 'Drive clinical management' column because its output — a triage flag indicating possible MI — is intended to directly inform the urgency and pathway of clinical decisions, including the prioritisation of reperfusion assessment. The healthcare situation is classified as 'Serious' because acute myocardial infarction represents a condition where accurate and timely information is critical to prevent serious deterioration or death, but is not immediately life-threatening in the same sense as, for example, a continuous critical care alarm. The intersection of 'Drive clinical management' and 'Serious' corresponds to IMDRF Category III, indicating a high-risk SaMD requiring robust clinical evidence and quality system controls.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under India's Medical Devices Rules, 2017, and the CDSCO risk classification framework, Class C encompasses devices that present a high potential risk to the patient or user. A Class C classification for CardioAI Triage is consistent with its IMDRF Category III designation and is further supported by the following: the device drives clinical management of a serious and time-critical cardiac condition; errors in output (false negatives in particular) may result in delayed treatment with serious patient harm; and the AI/ML-based operation introduces additional performance uncertainty relative to deterministic algorithms. The October 2025 CDSCO Draft SaMD Guidance is expected to codify Class C as the appropriate designation for AI-CDS SaMD operating in serious clinical states. A central CDSCO pathway via the Central Licensing Authority is therefore the applicable route.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Acute myocardial infarction remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in India, with time-to-treatment being a critical determinant of outcomes. In high-volume emergency departments and resource-constrained settings, ECG interpretation delays — due to limited specialist availability — contribute to treatment lag. CardioAI Triage addresses this gap by providing an immediate, AI-assisted triage signal to frontline clinical staff, enabling faster escalation to cardiologist review and potential reperfusion pathways. The device is positioned as a decision-support adjunct rather than a replacement for specialist interpretation, with particular relevance in tier-2 and tier-3 hospitals where cardiologist access may be limited during off-hours.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Several international AI-ECG products have received regulatory clearance in analogous indications, including FDA 510(k)-cleared and CE-marked ECG analysis SaMDs for MI detection. Notable examples include AI-driven ECG interpretation platforms from AliveCor, Eko Health, and Cardiologs, as well as ECG-MI detection modules integrated into major ECG hardware manufacturers' software suites. At the time of this submission, no directly equivalent AI-ECG triage SaMD is confirmed to hold a CDSCO marketing authorization in India, which may require the applicant to rely on international regulatory precedents and independently generated clinical evidence to establish substantial equivalence or safety and performance benchmarks.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence plan for CardioAI Triage is anticipated to comprise two sequential phases. Phase 1 involves a retrospective analytical validation study using a cardiologist-adjudicated, de-identified 12-lead ECG dataset representative of the Indian population, evaluating sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and AUC for MI detection against ground-truth labels. This study should be conducted at a NABL-accredited or equivalent institution and reviewed by an independent ethics committee. Phase 2 involves a prospective clinical investigation registered on CTRI and conducted under the CDSCO MD-22/MD-23 framework at a minimum of two sites, assessing real-world clinical decision impact, device safety, and subgroup performance. Publication in a peer-reviewed journal is advisable to support CDSCO technical file requirements.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is recommended for CardioAI Triage given its AI/ML-based inference engine. The PCCP should define the categories of anticipated model changes — including periodic retraining on expanded ECG datasets, threshold recalibration based on post-market performance data, and architectural updates — and specify for each change type whether it constitutes a modification requiring a new regulatory submission or falls within pre-approved change boundaries. The plan should be aligned with IMDRF guidance on SaMD modifications and the principles outlined in the CDSCO October 2025 Draft SaMD Guidance once finalised. Each change boundary should be defined by quantitative performance thresholds (e.g., sensitivity/specificity tolerance limits) and the scope of population or indication coverage.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change control protocol should specify a tiered review process: minor changes (e.g., bug fixes, UI updates with no impact on AI output) managed through internal IEC 62304-compliant change control; moderate changes (e.g., retraining on additional data within the validated distribution) reviewed by the internal clinical and regulatory affairs committee with documented performance benchmarking; and major changes (e.g., new intended use, new patient population, significant architectural modification) routed through a formal CDSCO variation or fresh application process. All changes should be logged in a version-controlled change register with traceability to risk management files under ISO 14971 and software lifecycle documentation under IEC 62304.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: A post-market performance monitoring (PMPM) programme should be established at deployment, capturing real-world metrics including model output distribution, clinician override rates, false-negative and false-positive event reports, and any adverse events or near-misses attributable to device output. Data should be reviewed on a defined periodic schedule (at minimum semi-annually) by a designated clinical reviewer. Feedback loops from monitoring should trigger predefined performance review criteria within the PCCP. Patient-level outcome data, where accessible through partner institutions, should be incorporated to assess clinical impact over time. All monitoring activities are expected to align with CDSCO post-market surveillance obligations under MDR 2017 and relevant provisions of the finalised SaMD guidance.

executive_summary.body:
SurgiPlan XR is an extended-reality (XR) surgical planning software intended to assist orthopedic surgeons in pre-operative planning of implant placement. Because the software is designed to influence clinical management decisions — specifically the position, size, and orientation of an orthopedic implant — in what is typically a serious clinical state, it is likely to fall within the medical device perimeter under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft framework. Mapped against the IMDRF SaMD risk framework, the device appears to sit in Category III (drives clinical management × serious situation), which corresponds to a likely CDSCO Class C designation. The presence of AI/ML components in the planning logic is expected to bring the product within the scope of the draft Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) expectations.

The expected regulatory pathway is a domestic manufacturing route through the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, with an MD-12 test license to support clinical investigation followed by an MD-9 manufacturing license. Given that no clear predicate has been identified, the dossier may need to demonstrate safety and performance through a prospective, Ethics-Committee-approved clinical validation aligned with ICMR's 2023 AI-in-healthcare guidelines. DPDP Act obligations are likely to apply given the handling of patient imaging and surgical planning data.

Current readiness is assessed at 1/10. Foundational elements — ISO 13485 QMS, IEC 62304 software lifecycle, ISO 14971 risk management, and a clinical validation plan — are not yet in place. A realistic timeline to a submission-ready dossier is 18–28 months, anchored to QMS build-out, software lifecycle documentation, EC-approved clinical validation, and central CDSCO interaction. This Draft Pack is intended as a starting point for the founder's own submission preparation in consultation with a qualified Indian regulatory consultant.
executive_summary.product_class: Likely Class C AI-driven Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing route — MD-12 test license to support clinical investigation, followed by MD-9 manufacturing license application to the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority
headline_gaps: Engage an ISO 13485 consultant for a Class C SaMD-scoped QMS gap assessment.
Stand up an IEC 62304-aligned software lifecycle (likely Safety Class B or C) with ISO 14971 risk management.
Identify 1–2 orthopedic sites and begin EC protocol drafting for a prospective clinical validation study.

intended_use.indication: SurgiPlan XR is intended for use as a pre-operative planning aid for orthopedic surgical procedures involving implant placement. It is intended to assist the operating surgeon in visualising patient-specific anatomy in an extended-reality environment and in evaluating candidate implant positions, sizes, and orientations prior to surgery. The output is intended to inform, but not replace, the surgeon's clinical judgement regarding the final surgical plan.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended user is a qualified orthopedic surgeon, or a surgical planning team working under the direct supervision of such a surgeon, with appropriate training in the interpretation of patient imaging and in the use of XR-based planning tools. The product is not intended for use by patients or by untrained personnel.
intended_use.use_environment: The product is intended for use in a hospital or surgical-centre setting, typically within a pre-operative planning workflow. It is expected to be used on supported XR hardware and computing platforms in a non-sterile pre-operative environment. It is not intended to be used intra-operatively as a real-time navigation system unless that indication is separately validated and cleared.
intended_use.contraindications: The product is not intended for use as the sole basis of a surgical decision, for emergency or unplanned procedures where appropriate imaging is unavailable, for patient populations or anatomical regions outside those covered by the validation dataset, or in clinical situations where required input imaging cannot be obtained at the specified quality. Use outside the validated indications may produce unreliable planning outputs.

device_description.components_architecture: SurgiPlan XR is expected to comprise a software application with three principal layers: (i) a data ingestion and pre-processing module that imports patient imaging (typically CT or MRI in DICOM format) and performs segmentation and registration; (ii) a planning engine, including AI/ML-based components, that proposes candidate implant placements based on segmented anatomy and a parameterised implant library; and (iii) an XR visualisation layer that renders the patient anatomy and candidate plans to the surgeon on supported headset or display hardware. Supporting components are expected to include user authentication, audit logging, and a planning-record export module.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software ingests patient-specific imaging, reconstructs a three-dimensional model of the relevant anatomy, and presents this model in an XR environment. AI/ML components may be used to assist with anatomical segmentation and with the generation of candidate implant placements. The surgeon reviews, manipulates, and approves the plan within the XR environment. The approved plan is exported as a record that the surgeon may reference during the procedure. The software does not, in its current intended use, drive any actuator or perform any automated intra-operative action.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only product, SurgiPlan XR has no patient-contacting materials. Development is expected to be aligned with IEC 62304 for software lifecycle processes (likely Safety Class B or C), ISO 14971 for risk management, ISO 13485 for the quality management system, IEC 62366-1 for usability engineering, and applicable parts of IEC 82304-1 for health software. Cybersecurity considerations are expected to follow IMDRF and CDSCO published guidance. Compatibility with referenced XR hardware will be specified in the technical file.
device_description.variants_accessories: Variants may include configurations for different anatomical regions (for example, hip, knee, or spine) and for different XR hardware platforms. Each variant is expected to be separately characterised in the technical file with respect to validated indications, supported imaging inputs, and supported implant libraries. Accessories are limited to commercially available XR display hardware specified by the manufacturer.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Software lifecycle activities are expected to follow IEC 62304, including planned releases, change control, and post-market software maintenance. End-of-support and decommissioning procedures will be defined, including secure deletion of patient data in line with DPDP Act obligations. As a software product, there is no physical disposal requirement; however, any locally stored patient data on user hardware will be subject to documented deletion procedures at end of use.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: SurgiPlan XR generates patient-specific implant placement plans that the surgeon uses to determine where and how an orthopedic implant is positioned. Under the IMDRF SaMD framework, software whose output is used to drive a clinical management decision — as opposed to merely informing or driving clinical investigation — falls into the 'drive' significance tier. Orthopedic implant procedures are typically performed in patients with significant musculoskeletal pathology where an incorrect plan could materially affect surgical outcomes, requiring corrective intervention; this is consistent with a 'serious' healthcare situation rather than a 'non-serious' or 'critical' one. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' and 'serious situation' maps to IMDRF Category III.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft framework, software falling in IMDRF Category III is generally expected to align with CDSCO Class C (moderate-high risk). The product's role in driving an invasive, implant-based surgical procedure, combined with the presence of AI/ML components in the planning logic, supports a Class C call rather than Class B. A Class D call would typically be reserved for software directly sustaining or supporting life. The Class C designation implies a central CDSCO licensing route (MD-9 with prior MD-12 for clinical investigation) and is expected to bring the product within the scope of the draft Algorithm Change Protocol expectations. This designation should be confirmed with CDSCO during a pre-submission interaction.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Orthopedic implant procedures, including arthroplasty and trauma fixation, depend heavily on accurate pre-operative planning of implant size, position, and orientation. Suboptimal planning is associated with increased revision rates, longer operative times, and poorer functional outcomes. Conventional 2D planning on radiographs has known limitations in three-dimensional anatomy. XR-based planning is intended to address this gap by allowing the surgeon to interact with a patient-specific 3D model and to evaluate candidate plans before incision, with the goal of improving plan accuracy and reducing intra-operative variability.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No clear Indian predicate has been identified at this stage. Internationally, several 3D and XR-based orthopedic planning tools have been cleared through other jurisdictions; these may serve as reference devices for benchmarking but are unlikely to function as formal predicates within the CDSCO framework. The dossier is therefore likely to be developed on a novel-device basis, with safety and performance demonstrated through direct clinical and bench evidence rather than substantial-equivalence arguments.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy is expected to combine: (i) verification and validation of segmentation and planning algorithms against curated, anatomically diverse imaging datasets, with performance metrics pre-specified; (ii) usability evaluation with representative orthopedic surgeons under IEC 62366-1; and (iii) a prospective, Ethics-Committee-approved clinical validation study at 1–2 Indian orthopedic sites, comparing planning accuracy and relevant clinical endpoints against the current standard planning workflow. The study design is expected to be aligned with ICMR's 2023 AI-in-healthcare ethical guidelines and with the New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules, 2019, as applicable to medical device investigations.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan is expected to be developed describing the categories of algorithm changes the manufacturer anticipates over the product lifecycle. These may include re-training of segmentation models on expanded imaging datasets, refinement of candidate-plan generation logic, and extension to additional anatomical regions or implant libraries. For each category, the PCCP is expected to specify the permitted scope of change, the input data characteristics, the acceptance criteria, and the boundary beyond which a fresh CDSCO interaction would be sought.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change protocol component is expected to define the procedural controls governing each anticipated change, including: data governance and curation requirements, model training and re-training procedures under IEC 62304 change control, verification and validation activities and their pre-specified pass criteria, risk-management updates under ISO 14971, usability re-assessment where relevant, and documentation and version-control practices. Changes outside the pre-specified envelope are expected to be handled as conventional design changes with appropriate regulatory notification.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: A post-market performance monitoring plan is expected to track real-world planning accuracy, surgeon overrides of system-proposed plans, adverse events potentially attributable to planning errors, and dataset drift indicators where feasible. Periodic performance reviews are expected to feed back into the PCCP cycle and into the manufacturer's post-market surveillance reports under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. Triggers for unscheduled review — such as a defined deterioration in monitored metrics — are expected to be pre-specified.

executive_summary.body:
SurgiPlan XR is an extended-reality (XR) software platform intended to support orthopedic surgeons in planning implant placement procedures. By overlaying patient-specific imaging data onto an interactive three-dimensional environment, the software is designed to assist in determining implant type, size, and positioning prior to surgical intervention. Because the outputs of this planning process are expected to directly inform intraoperative decisions, SurgiPlan XR is likely classified as a Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR 2017) and the emerging CDSCO SaMD draft guidance. The combination of a serious clinical situation — orthopedic surgery — and a function that drives clinical management maps the product to IMDRF SaMD Category III, which in turn corresponds to CDSCO Class C.

The product incorporates AI/ML components that generate or refine implant placement recommendations. This introduces obligations under CDSCO's October 2025 draft guidance on AI-based medical devices, including the likely requirement for a Pre-determined Change Control Plan (PCCP) and an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP). No predicate device has been identified within the Indian regulatory landscape, which increases scrutiny and extends the anticipated review timeline.

Current regulatory readiness is assessed as very low (score: 1 out of 10). No Quality Management System (QMS) aligned to ISO 13485 has been detected, no IEC 62304 software lifecycle documentation is in place, and no clinical validation pathway is active. These foundational elements are prerequisites for any viable CDSCO submission. The anticipated pathway involves an MD-12 test license to support clinical investigation, followed by a central licensing application under MD-9 for market authorization as a Class C SaMD. The estimated timeline from foundational build-out to submission is 18–28 months, contingent on parallel QMS implementation, software lifecycle documentation, and Ethics Committee-approved clinical validation.
executive_summary.product_class: Class C SaMD (AI-CDS, novel, no Indian predicate)
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic SaMD pathway — MD-12 test license application to support Ethics Committee-approved clinical investigation, followed by MD-9 central licensing authority application for Class C SaMD market authorization; Algorithm Change Protocol submission expected under October 2025 CDSCO AI/SaMD draft guidance
headline_gaps: Initiate ISO 13485 QMS gap assessment immediately with a consultant scoped to Class C SaMD manufacturing obligations.
Establish an IEC 62304-aligned software lifecycle process (Safety Class B or C) with integrated ISO 14971 risk management documentation.
Engage 1–2 orthopedic surgery sites and begin Ethics Committee protocol drafting for a prospective clinical validation study.

intended_use.indication: SurgiPlan XR is intended to assist qualified orthopedic surgeons in pre-operative surgical planning for orthopedic implant placement procedures. The software processes patient-specific imaging data — including radiographic and/or computed tomography datasets — and renders an interactive three-dimensional extended-reality environment to support the selection, sizing, and positional planning of orthopedic implants. The device is intended as a planning aid and is expected to be used in conjunction with, not as a replacement for, the surgeon's clinical judgment and established surgical protocols.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended primary users are licensed orthopedic surgeons and qualified surgical planning specialists operating within accredited hospital or surgical center environments. Secondary users may include orthopedic surgical residents operating under direct consultant supervision. The device is not intended for use by patients, general practitioners, or non-clinical personnel. Users are expected to have familiarity with orthopedic anatomy, standard imaging modalities, and implant selection criteria as part of their clinical training.
intended_use.use_environment: SurgiPlan XR is intended to be used in pre-operative planning settings, typically within hospital surgical departments, imaging suites, or dedicated pre-operative assessment rooms equipped with compatible XR hardware and imaging infrastructure. The software is not intended for direct intraoperative use on the sterile field, though planning outputs may be referenced in the operating theater. The use environment is expected to include controlled lighting conditions and institutional-grade network connectivity sufficient to support imaging data retrieval and XR rendering.
intended_use.contraindications: SurgiPlan XR is not indicated as a standalone decision-making tool; all planning outputs are expected to be reviewed and validated by a qualified orthopedic surgeon prior to surgical execution. The device is not intended for use in emergency or acute surgical scenarios where pre-operative planning time is not available. Use with imaging datasets that do not meet minimum resolution or quality thresholds specified in the device labeling may produce unreliable planning outputs and is not recommended. The device is not indicated for non-orthopedic surgical planning applications.

device_description.components_architecture: SurgiPlan XR comprises three principal components: (1) an imaging data ingestion and processing module that accepts standard DICOM-format radiographic and CT datasets from hospital PACS or portable media; (2) an AI/ML inference engine that analyzes bone morphology, joint geometry, and patient-specific anatomical parameters to generate implant sizing and positioning recommendations; and (3) an XR rendering and interaction layer delivered through a compatible head-mounted display or mixed-reality device, presenting the surgeon with an interactive three-dimensional pre-operative planning workspace. The software architecture is expected to be deployed as an on-premise installation or a secured private-cloud configuration within hospital IT environments, with role-based access controls and audit logging.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The device operates by segmenting and reconstructing patient-specific skeletal anatomy from uploaded imaging data. The AI/ML component applies trained models — developed on curated orthopedic imaging datasets — to identify anatomical landmarks, estimate bone quality regions, and propose candidate implant configurations from a linked implant library. These candidate configurations are rendered in the XR environment, allowing the surgeon to visualize, rotate, and iteratively adjust implant placement in three dimensions. Surgeon-confirmed planning outputs are expected to be exportable as structured reports for pre-operative documentation. The AI component is designed to function as a decision-support tool, with all final planning decisions remaining with the operating surgeon.
device_description.materials_standards: SurgiPlan XR is a software-only device; no physical materials or biocompatible substances are incorporated. The software is expected to be developed in conformance with IEC 62304 (Medical device software — Software life cycle processes) at Safety Class B or C, commensurate with the risk level associated with driving surgical decision-making. Cybersecurity design is expected to align with IEC 81001-5-1 and CDSCO cybersecurity guidance applicable to SaMD. DICOM conformance is expected to be documented in a formal DICOM Conformance Statement. Usability engineering is expected to follow IEC 62366-1.
device_description.variants_accessories: At the time of this submission draft, SurgiPlan XR is described as a single software product. Should future variants be introduced — such as modules supporting additional anatomical regions (e.g., spine, shoulder) or integration with specific XR hardware platforms — each variant is expected to be assessed for its impact on the device's risk classification and may require separate or supplementary regulatory submissions. Compatible third-party XR headsets and hospital PACS systems are considered accessories; their specifications and validated compatibility configurations are expected to be documented in the technical file.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: As a software-only device, SurgiPlan XR does not involve physical disposal considerations. The software lifecycle is expected to be managed under an IEC 62304-aligned Software Development and Maintenance Plan, covering version control, planned updates, defect management, and end-of-support policies. Post-market surveillance activities, including monitoring of planning output accuracy and user-reported anomalies, are expected to feed into periodic safety update reporting as required under MDR 2017 for Class C devices. Decommissioning procedures are expected to address secure deletion of patient imaging data in compliance with DPDP Act obligations.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Framework, risk category is determined by the intersection of the significance of information provided by the SaMD and the healthcare situation or condition to which it is applied. SurgiPlan XR is designed to generate implant size, type, and positional recommendations that are directly intended to inform a surgeon's pre-operative plan — a function that meets the IMDRF definition of 'drive clinical management,' as the output is expected to guide a specific therapeutic intervention. Orthopedic implant surgery represents a serious healthcare situation, defined by IMDRF as a condition that, if managed suboptimally, may result in significant morbidity, functional impairment, or require revision surgery. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' and 'serious' condition maps unambiguously to IMDRF SaMD Category III.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017, Schedule V, Rule 13, medical devices are classified A through D based on risk, intended purpose, and duration of contact or influence. SaMD that drives clinical management decisions in a serious condition is expected to correspond to Class C under the CDSCO SaMD framework, which aligns with intermediate-to-high risk devices requiring central licensing authority approval. IMDRF Category III is the recognized basis for this mapping under CDSCO's published and draft SaMD guidance. The AI/ML character of the inference engine further elevates regulatory scrutiny, as the October 2025 CDSCO AI-based SaMD draft introduces additional obligations — including an Algorithm Change Protocol — for software that incorporates adaptive or trained model components. No Indian predicate device has been identified, which is expected to increase the evidentiary requirements for clinical validation in the submission dossier.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Orthopedic implant surgery — including total and partial joint replacement, fracture fixation, and corrective osteotomy — requires precise pre-operative planning to optimize implant fit, alignment, and biomechanical outcomes. Conventional two-dimensional radiographic templating is associated with recognized limitations in accounting for patient-specific three-dimensional anatomy, bone quality variation, and implant-bone interface geometry. Suboptimal implant sizing or positioning may contribute to early implant failure, periprosthetic fracture, instability, or the need for revision surgery, each of which carries significant morbidity and healthcare cost burden. XR-based three-dimensional planning tools address this gap by enabling surgeons to interact with patient-specific anatomy in an immersive environment, potentially reducing planning errors and improving intraoperative efficiency. In the Indian context, the growing volume of orthopedic procedures in both public and private hospital systems creates a significant addressable clinical need for validated surgical planning technology.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No direct Indian regulatory predicate for an XR-based AI-assisted orthopedic surgical planning SaMD has been identified in published CDSCO device registration records at the time of this draft. Internationally, analogous software products — such as two-dimensional digital templating tools and three-dimensional pre-operative planning platforms for joint replacement — have received regulatory clearance in jurisdictions including the US FDA (510(k) pathway) and CE-marked under the EU MDR. These international clearances may be referenced as supporting evidence of the device category's established clinical utility; however, they are not expected to substitute for Indian clinical validation data in a CDSCO Class C submission. The novel nature of this product within the Indian regulatory landscape is expected to necessitate a more comprehensive clinical evidence package.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy for SurgiPlan XR is expected to comprise two primary components. First, a prospective comparative validation study is anticipated, conducted at a minimum of one to two NABH-accredited orthopedic surgery centers in India, comparing planning outputs generated by SurgiPlan XR against current standard-of-care planning workflows. Primary endpoints are expected to include implant size prediction accuracy (concordance with intraoperatively implanted size), planning time, and user-rated usability. An Ethics Committee-approved protocol aligned with ICMR's 2023 AI-in-healthcare guidelines and Good Clinical Practice standards is expected to be a prerequisite for the MD-12 test license application. Second, a post-market clinical follow-up plan is expected to be included in the MD-9 submission, describing ongoing real-world performance monitoring, adverse event reporting, and periodic clinical evidence review in alignment with MDR 2017 requirements for Class C devices.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Pre-determined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to be developed and submitted as part of the SurgiPlan XR regulatory dossier, in alignment with CDSCO's October 2025 draft guidance on AI/ML-based SaMD. The PCCP is anticipated to describe the categories of algorithm modifications that may be implemented post-market without requiring a full new submission — specifically, performance re-training on expanded orthopedic imaging datasets within pre-defined anatomical scope, and model recalibration for new implant library entries. Each change category is expected to be bounded by explicit performance boundary conditions, including minimum acceptable implant size prediction accuracy thresholds and maximum permissible regression in validated anatomical landmark detection metrics. Changes falling outside these boundaries are expected to trigger a supplementary submission to CDSCO.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) for SurgiPlan XR is expected to define the governance framework for managing the AI inference engine across its post-market lifecycle. The protocol is anticipated to specify: (1) version control and configuration management procedures for trained model artifacts, aligned with IEC 62304 change management requirements; (2) a re-validation workflow that requires internal bench testing, followed by prospective clinical site testing for any change meeting the PCCP boundary trigger conditions; (3) a risk assessment gate under ISO 14971 for each algorithm update, evaluating the potential for introduction of new failure modes or degradation of safety-critical outputs; and (4) a regulatory notification decision tree mapping the nature and magnitude of each change to the appropriate CDSCO reporting obligation under MDR 2017 and the applicable SaMD draft guidance.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring for the AI component of SurgiPlan XR is expected to be operationalized through a structured data collection mechanism embedded in the software's deployment environment. Surgeons are expected to be prompted to record the final implanted size and any intraoperative deviations from the pre-operative plan, creating a feedback dataset for ongoing model performance evaluation. Key performance indicators — including implant size concordance rate, planning revision frequency, and user-reported anomaly flags — are expected to be reviewed at defined intervals (anticipated quarterly for the first two post-launch years). Significant performance drift or clustering of adverse feedback is expected to trigger a formal CAPA under the ISO 13485 QMS and, where appropriate, a regulatory field safety notice or supplementary submission to CDSCO. All patient data collected for monitoring purposes is expected to be processed in conformance with DPDP Act obligations.

executive_summary.body:
StrokeFast is an AI-based diagnostic Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) intended to analyze non-contrast CT brain imaging and flag findings suggestive of acute stroke to support timely clinical decision-making in emergency and radiology workflows. Based on the IMDRF SaMD framework, the product is expected to drive clinical management in a serious-to-critical healthcare situation, placing it at IMDRF Category III. Under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017, read with the October 2025 draft guidance on SaMD, this likely corresponds to Class C, with the Central Licensing Authority as the competent authority.

The anticipated regulatory pathway involves an MD-12 test license to support clinical investigation, followed by an MD-9 manufacturing license for domestic manufacture and distribution.
