Because the device incorporates a machine-learning algorithm whose performance may be updated post-deployment, an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) aligned to the CDSCO draft and IMDRF Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) principles is expected to form part of the submission. Parallel obligations under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 apply to the processing of CT imaging data, and ICMR 2023 AI guidelines apply to the clinical validation strategy.

Current readiness is at a pre-foundation stage. No ISO 13485 quality management system or IEC 62304 software lifecycle evidence has been documented, no Ethics Committee–approved clinical validation has been initiated, and no formal CDSCO engagement has occurred. A realistic timeline to a defensible MD-9 submission is in the range of 18–28 months, contingent on parallel build-out of the QMS, technical documentation, and a prospective clinical validation study at partner stroke-care centres. The immediate priority is to establish the quality system foundation, plan an EC-approved validation cohort, and initiate pre-submission dialogue with CDSCO to socialize the SaMD classification and ACP design early in the lifecycle.

intended_use.indication: StrokeFast is intended as a computer-assisted triage and diagnostic support software that analyzes non-contrast CT brain images of adult patients presenting with suspected acute stroke. The software identifies image features suggestive of ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke and surfaces these findings to support timely radiologist review and clinical decision-making. The output is adjunctive and is not intended to replace independent radiologist interpretation or definitive clinical diagnosis.
intended_use.intended_user: The primary users are likely to be radiologists, neurologists, and emergency physicians operating in hospital radiology and emergency department settings. Use is expected to be limited to qualified medical practitioners who interpret CT brain imaging as part of routine clinical practice. Hospital IT and PACS administrators may interact with the software for deployment and workflow integration but are not clinical end-users.
intended_use.use_environment: The software is expected to be deployed within hospital radiology information systems and PACS environments, including tertiary care centres, stroke-ready hospitals, and emergency imaging units. Deployment may be on-premise or via secure cloud infrastructure compliant with DPDP 2023 obligations. The software is intended for use in environments with standard CT acquisition protocols and reliable network connectivity to imaging archives.
intended_use.contraindications: The software is not intended for use on paediatric patients, on imaging modalities other than non-contrast CT brain (such as MRI or CT angiography), or as a sole basis for definitive stroke diagnosis or treatment decisions. It is not validated for image data acquired with non-standard protocols, severe motion artefact, or significant prior craniotomy or intracranial hardware that may confound algorithmic interpretation. The output is likely contraindicated as a standalone decision tool in the absence of qualified clinician review.

device_description.components_architecture: StrokeFast is a standalone software medical device comprising a deep-learning inference engine, a DICOM ingestion and pre-processing pipeline, a results-presentation module, and an audit-logging subsystem. The software is expected to integrate with hospital PACS via standard DICOM interfaces and may be deployed on-premise or via a secure cloud-hosted service. There are no hardware components manufactured by the licence holder; the device operates on general-purpose computing infrastructure meeting documented minimum specifications.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software receives non-contrast CT brain studies in DICOM format, applies pre-processing (normalization, skull-stripping, slice alignment), and runs a trained convolutional neural network to detect imaging features associated with ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke. The model outputs a probability score and region-of-interest overlays that are presented to the interpreting clinician through a viewer or PACS-integrated worklist notification. The principle of operation is pattern recognition over labelled imaging datasets, with model performance bounded by the training distribution and validated on held-out and prospective cohorts.
device_description.materials_standards: As a SaMD, no biological or patient-contact materials are involved. The development and lifecycle are expected to be aligned with IEC 62304 (software lifecycle), ISO 14971 (risk management), ISO 13485 (QMS), IEC 82304-1 (health software product safety), and applicable parts of IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering). Cybersecurity is expected to be addressed in line with IMDRF and CDSCO cybersecurity guidance, and data protection in line with DPDP 2023.
device_description.variants_accessories: Initial scope is expected to include a single software product variant supporting non-contrast CT brain analysis. Future variants covering additional modalities or clinical indications would likely be filed as separate or amended submissions. There are no physical accessories. Optional integration components (such as PACS connectors or hospital deployment agents) may be specified as configuration options rather than separate accessories.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Software lifecycle activities are expected to follow IEC 62304, including planned releases, patch management, and post-market surveillance. End-of-life and decommissioning procedures would typically include secure deletion of cached imaging data, revocation of deployment licences, and notification to deployed sites with sufficient lead time. Imaging data handling at end-of-life is expected to align with DPDP 2023 retention and erasure obligations.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Acute stroke is a time-critical, life-threatening clinical condition where intervention windows are measured in hours. The software's output—flagging suspected stroke on CT brain imaging—is intended to drive urgent clinical management, including triage prioritization, downstream imaging, and treatment decisions such as thrombolysis or thrombectomy referral. Under the IMDRF SaMD framework, software that drives clinical management in a critical healthcare situation maps to Category III. While the output is adjunctive and a qualified clinician retains final interpretation responsibility, the practical clinical workflow impact and the severity of false-negative or false-positive outputs justify the Category III placement rather than a lower significance tier.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules 2017, software intended for diagnosis is classified by reference to its intended use and the risk associated with incorrect output. The CDSCO October 2025 draft guidance on SaMD aligns Indian classification with the IMDRF framework, placing IMDRF Category III SaMD at Class C. Diagnostic AI software for a serious-to-critical condition such as stroke is therefore expected to be regulated as a Class C medical device, with the Central Licensing Authority as the competent authority. This places the product within the MD-9 manufacturing licence pathway, with an MD-12 test licence likely required to support the clinical investigation that underpins the safety and performance dossier.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Stroke is a leading cause of death and long-term disability in India, with outcomes strongly dependent on time-to-treatment. Many Indian hospitals, particularly outside major metros, face shortages of on-call neuro-radiology expertise during off-hours, leading to delays in CT interpretation and treatment initiation. AI-based triage and diagnostic-support tools are positioned to reduce time-to-diagnosis, prioritize suspected stroke cases on radiologist worklists, and support standardized interpretation across sites. The clinical need is well-recognized in Indian stroke-care pathways and aligns with the priorities articulated by the Indian Stroke Association and tertiary stroke centres.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Internationally, several AI stroke-detection SaMDs have received regulatory clearances in comparable jurisdictions, providing reference points for intended use, performance benchmarks, and labelling. India does not operate a formal predicate-device system equivalent to the US 510(k) framework; however, prior CDSCO clearances of imaging-AI SaMD (where available) and international clearances may be cited as supportive reference. A formal predicate analysis is expected to be developed during the dossier-preparation phase and discussed with CDSCO during pre-submission consultation.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy is expected to comprise three layers: (i) retrospective analytical validation on a labelled, multi-site Indian CT dataset covering ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke and stroke-mimics, with sensitivity, specificity, and AUC reporting; (ii) a prospective Ethics Committee–approved clinical investigation at 2–3 partner stroke centres, registered on CTRI, conducted in alignment with ICMR 2023 AI guidelines and the New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules 2019 as applicable; and (iii) a post-market real-world performance monitoring plan tied to the Algorithm Change Protocol. The MD-12 test licence is likely to be the gateway to the prospective study.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan is expected to define the scope of permitted post-market algorithm modifications without requiring a fresh submission. Likely in-scope changes include retraining on additional Indian site data, threshold recalibration, and minor architecture updates that preserve intended use. Out-of-scope changes—such as expansion to new modalities (MRI, CTA), new indications, or changes that alter the IMDRF category—would typically trigger a variation or fresh application. The PCCP is expected to be aligned with IMDRF good machine-learning practice and the CDSCO October 2025 draft guidance.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change protocol is expected to specify, for each in-scope change type, the data requirements, performance acceptance criteria, verification and validation activities, documentation updates, and internal governance approvals before deployment. It would typically reference IEC 62304 software change-control processes, ISO 14971 risk re-assessment, and a defined regression-testing suite covering stroke subtypes, demographic strata, and scanner-vendor variation. Each algorithm release is expected to be logged with a version identifier, training data snapshot, validation report, and labelling impact assessment retained in the technical file.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world monitoring is expected to include continuous tracking of model performance against radiologist final reports at deployed sites, drift-detection metrics, false-positive and false-negative case review, and a structured complaint and adverse-event pathway feeding into post-market surveillance reports. Monitoring outputs would inform PCCP-governed retraining cycles and any required field-safety actions. Reporting cadence, KPIs, and trigger thresholds for regulator notification are expected to be defined in the surveillance plan and shared with CDSCO as part of the ACP submission package.

executive_summary.body:
StrokeFast is an artificial-intelligence-powered Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) that analyzes non-contrast CT brain images to detect findings consistent with acute stroke. The product is intended for use within hospital radiology and emergency neurology workflows, where timely identification of stroke is clinically critical. Based on the IMDRF SaMD risk framework, StrokeFast is classified at IMDRF Category III, reflecting its role in driving clinical management decisions in a serious healthcare situation. Under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR 2017) and the CDSCO Draft SaMD Guidance (October 2025), this product is expected to attract a Class C designation, subject to central licensing authority oversight.

The regulatory pathway likely involves an MD-12 test license to cover the clinical investigation phase, followed by a central manufacturing or import license application (MD-9 for domestic; MD-14 for imported software). Because StrokeFast incorporates an adaptive AI/ML algorithm, a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) and Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) are expected components of the technical submission dossier.

The company's current readiness is assessed at the pre-foundation stage. No evidence of an operational ISO 13485 quality management system, IEC 62304-compliant software lifecycle documentation, Ethics Committee-approved clinical validation study, or formal CDSCO engagement has been identified. These absences represent the primary barriers to submission readiness. The realistic time horizon to a submission-ready dossier is estimated at 18–28 months, contingent on parallel workstreams across quality system build-out, prospective clinical validation, and iterative pre-submission engagement with CDSCO. Founders are strongly advised to prioritize QMS establishment and early CDSCO socialization before committing resources to dossier compilation.
executive_summary.product_class: Class C SaMD (AI-CDS, IMDRF Category III)
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic SaMD — MD-12 test license for Ethics Committee-approved clinical investigation, followed by central MD-9 manufacturing/import license application to CDSCO Central Licensing Authority; Predetermined Change Control Plan and ACP expected as part of the technical dossier under the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD Draft Guidance
headline_gaps: Establish ISO 13485 QMS and IEC 62304 software lifecycle controls immediately — these are foundational prerequisites for any Class C SaMD dossier.
Initiate Ethics Committee-approved clinical validation (retrospective cohort + prospective study, CTRI-registered) across 2–3 partner hospitals to generate the clinical evidence package.
Open a SUGAM account and request a CDSCO pre-submission consultation to socialize the SaMD classification, ACP plan, and intended pathway before dossier drafting begins.

intended_use.indication: StrokeFast is intended to analyze non-contrast computed tomography (NCCT) brain images in adult patients presenting with suspected acute stroke. The software processes DICOM-format CT imaging data to generate an AI-derived probability score and localization output indicating the likelihood of acute ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke findings. The output is intended to function as a decision-support aid, drawing clinician attention to findings that may warrant urgent evaluation. The device is not intended to replace clinical judgment, neurological examination, or confirmatory imaging.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended primary users are qualified radiologists and emergency physicians operating within hospital settings who have received appropriate training in the interpretation of CT brain imaging and in the use of AI-assisted diagnostic tools. Secondary users may include neuroradiologists and stroke neurologists reviewing flagged cases. The device is not intended for use by patients, caregivers, or non-clinical personnel.
intended_use.use_environment: StrokeFast is intended for deployment within hospital radiology departments, emergency departments, and stroke centers in inpatient and emergency care settings. The software is expected to integrate with existing Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS) infrastructure via standard DICOM interfaces. Use in low-resource or unmonitored ambulatory settings is outside the intended use scope. Network connectivity, compatible PACS hardware, and a qualified clinical reviewer are expected to be present at the point of use.
intended_use.contraindications: StrokeFast is not indicated for use as a standalone diagnostic device or as a substitute for radiologist interpretation. The device is not validated for pediatric populations, non-DICOM imaging formats, or CT acquisitions performed on scanner models not represented in the training and validation datasets. Use in patients with prior cranial surgery, large metallic implants producing significant artifact, or imaging acquired outside validated acquisition parameters may yield unreliable outputs and is outside the intended use. The device does not provide treatment recommendations and should not be used to guide thrombolysis or thrombectomy decisions without confirmatory clinical and imaging assessment.

device_description.components_architecture: StrokeFast comprises three principal software components: (1) a DICOM ingestion and pre-processing module responsible for image normalization, slice alignment, and quality filtering; (2) a deep-learning inference engine that applies a trained convolutional neural network (CNN) model to the processed CT volume to generate stroke probability scores and anatomical localization maps; and (3) a results presentation layer that renders outputs — including a numerical confidence score, annotated overlay images, and an alert flag — within the PACS viewer or a dedicated web-based dashboard accessible to the treating clinician. The system is expected to operate as server-side software deployed on-premise or in a private cloud environment within the hospital network.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The device operates on the principle of supervised deep learning applied to medical image analysis. During inference, a DICOM CT brain series is automatically retrieved from the PACS or uploaded via a secure interface. The pre-processing module standardizes window/level settings, resamples slice thickness, and removes non-brain structures. The inference engine applies a pre-trained CNN architecture — trained on labeled CT datasets encompassing ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke cases alongside normal studies — to generate voxel-level probability maps. These maps are aggregated to produce a patient-level stroke likelihood score. The output is presented as a prioritization flag and annotated image overlay, intended to direct clinician attention to high-probability findings for expedited review.
device_description.materials_standards: StrokeFast is a pure software device with no physical material components. The software development lifecycle is expected to conform to IEC 62304 (Medical device software — Software life cycle processes) and ISO 14971 (Application of risk management to medical devices). Algorithm development and validation practices are expected to align with the IMDRF SaMD Clinical Evaluation guidance (N41) and the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD Draft Guidance. Data security and privacy controls are expected to align with ISO 27001 and applicable DPDP Act, 2023 obligations. DICOM conformance is expected to meet NEMA DICOM standards for interoperability with hospital PACS infrastructure.
device_description.variants_accessories: At the time of this submission draft, StrokeFast is described as a single-variant product targeting NCCT brain imaging for acute stroke detection. No physical accessories are associated with the device. Future variants — such as modules for CT angiography (CTA) analysis or perfusion imaging — are outside the current intended use scope and would be expected to require separate regulatory evaluation. Any substantive algorithmic update or expansion of intended use is expected to be managed through the Predetermined Change Control Plan described in this dossier.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: As a software-only device, StrokeFast does not involve physical disposal considerations. The software lifecycle is expected to be governed by a documented software maintenance plan aligned with IEC 62304, covering scheduled version updates, security patching, and model performance monitoring. End-of-life decommissioning is expected to involve secure deletion of patient imaging data in accordance with the DPDP Act, 2023 and applicable hospital data governance policies. Version control and change history are expected to be maintained throughout the product lifecycle as part of the technical file and post-market surveillance records.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Framework (N12), risk category is determined by the intersection of the significance of the information provided and the healthcare situation. StrokeFast provides AI-generated diagnostic outputs that are intended to direct clinician attention to possible acute stroke — a finding that directly informs triage priority, imaging escalation, and time-critical treatment decisions such as thrombolysis eligibility. This places the significance of information at 'Drive clinical management.' Acute stroke represents a serious, potentially life-threatening and permanently debilitating condition, placing the healthcare situation at 'Serious.' The intersection of 'Drive clinical management' and 'Serious' maps to IMDRF SaMD Category III, indicating substantial potential for patient harm if the device provides incorrect outputs and clinical reliance on those outputs is not appropriately managed.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 and the CDSCO October 2025 Draft SaMD Guidance, SaMD products classified at IMDRF Category III are expected to attract a Class C designation. Class C devices are defined as those posing a moderate-to-high risk, where malfunction or incorrect output could cause serious injury or harm. StrokeFast, as an AI-driven diagnostic SaMD operating on a critical, time-sensitive condition, aligns with this risk band. The central licensing authority pathway is expected to apply, with technical documentation requirements including IEC 62304 software lifecycle evidence, ISO 14971 risk management file, a clinical investigation dossier, and an Algorithm Change Protocol. The classification is consistent with analogous AI diagnostic imaging SaMD products reviewed under comparable international frameworks (FDA, EU MDR Class IIb).

clinical_context.clinical_need: Stroke is among the leading causes of mortality and long-term disability in India, with time-to-diagnosis being a critical determinant of patient outcomes. The therapeutic window for intravenous thrombolysis is approximately 4.5 hours from symptom onset, placing significant pressure on emergency radiology workflows. Many Indian hospitals — particularly in Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities — experience radiologist shortages, high imaging volumes, and delayed after-hours reporting. StrokeFast addresses this gap by providing an automated, AI-assisted triage signal on NCCT brain studies, potentially reducing the time between image acquisition and clinician alert. The unmet need is strongest in hospital settings without 24/7 dedicated neuroradiology coverage, where a reliable AI flag may enable earlier escalation and expedited specialist review.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Several AI-based CT stroke detection platforms have received regulatory authorization in comparable jurisdictions, including Viz.ai (FDA 510(k) cleared, CE marked), Aidoc (FDA cleared, CE marked), and RapidAI Stroke (TGA registered, CE marked). These products share a substantially similar intended use — AI-assisted detection of acute stroke findings on CT brain imaging to support clinical triage — and operate via analogous PACS-integrated architectures. In the Indian market, no directly comparable product appears to have completed the CDSCO Class C SaMD pathway under the October 2025 draft; however, the existence of international predicates provides a basis for clinical performance benchmarking and evidentiary design. The company is advised to confirm predicate landscape with CDSCO during the pre-submission consultation.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence package is expected to include two phases. Phase 1 involves a retrospective validation study using archived, de-identified NCCT brain studies from 2–3 partner hospitals, with ground truth established by adjudicated radiologist reads and, where available, confirmatory MRI or discharge diagnosis. Primary performance endpoints are expected to include sensitivity, specificity, AUC, and time-to-alert metrics, stratified by stroke subtype (ischemic vs. hemorrhagic) and ASPECTS score range. Phase 2 involves an Ethics Committee-approved prospective observational study, registered on CTRI, to assess real-world performance and clinician workflow integration. The study protocol is expected to align with ICMR 2023 AI in Healthcare guidelines. Post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF) obligations under MDR 2017 are expected to be fulfilled through a structured real-world performance monitoring plan described in the Algorithm Change Protocol.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: StrokeFast's Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to define the boundaries within which algorithm updates may be implemented without requiring a full re-submission to CDSCO. The PCCP is anticipated to cover: (1) performance improvement updates where retrained model weights are applied to an architecturally identical CNN without change to intended use, input data type, or output format; (2) expansion of validated scanner models within the existing CT modality; and (3) software-level updates addressing security patches, UI changes, and DICOM compatibility improvements. Each change type is expected to be accompanied by pre-specified performance thresholds (e.g., sensitivity floor, specificity floor, AUC minimum) that must be met on a holdout validation dataset before deployment. Changes exceeding PCCP boundaries — including new indications, new imaging modalities, or architectural model changes — are expected to trigger a supplementary submission or new application as appropriate.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) is expected to document the governance process for all AI model updates throughout the product lifecycle. Key elements include: (1) a change classification matrix distinguishing minor (within PCCP), moderate (requiring internal technical review and CDSCO notification), and major (requiring supplementary submission) changes; (2) a validation protocol specifying minimum dataset size, demographic representation requirements, and statistical acceptance criteria for each change class; (3) a review and approval workflow involving the Quality Management System's design change control process, clinical reviewer sign-off, and regulatory affairs clearance prior to deployment; and (4) version-controlled documentation requirements linking each deployed model version to its corresponding validation evidence, risk assessment update, and software release record under IEC 62304.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-deployment performance monitoring is expected to be implemented through a continuous real-world performance surveillance framework. This framework is anticipated to include: (1) automated logging of AI output scores alongside anonymized case metadata (scanner model, acquisition parameters, patient demographics) to a secure monitoring database; (2) a clinician feedback mechanism enabling radiologists to flag cases where AI output was discordant with final diagnosis, forming the basis of an ongoing ground-truth reconciliation dataset; (3) quarterly statistical process control analysis of sensitivity, specificity, and false-positive rate trends, with pre-defined drift thresholds triggering formal model re-evaluation; and (4) an annual performance report submitted as part of post-market surveillance obligations under MDR 2017, with findings informing PCCP boundary review. Data collection and processing under this framework is expected to comply with the DPDP Act, 2023 and applicable hospital data sharing agreements.

executive_summary.body:
OncoPath Assist is an AI-based digital pathology software that analyses scanned histopathology slide images and suggests likely cancer grading to assist pathologists in oncology diagnostic workflows. Based on the Tier 0 readiness assessment and the IMDRF SaMD framework, the product is expected to be regulated as a Class C IVD-SaMD under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules, 2017, with additional expectations emerging from the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, including an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP).

The intended pathway is likely to involve a test licence under Form MD-12 to support clinical performance evaluation, followed by a manufacturing licence under Form MD-9 from the Central Licensing Authority (assuming domestic manufacturing). Clinical performance evaluation is expected to be conducted in collaboration with NABL-accredited (ISO 15189) pathology laboratories under ICMR's 2023 AI-in-healthcare guidance and EC-approved study protocols. Patient slide data handling is expected to fall within the scope of the DPDP Act, 2023.

Current readiness is at a foundational stage (1/10 in the Tier 0 card). No ISO 13485 quality management system or IEC 62304 software lifecycle process has been detected, no NABL-accredited laboratory partnership has been documented, and no CDSCO engagement has been initiated. These are typical baseline expectations for a Class C SaMD submission and likely need to be addressed before MD-12 drafting.

The estimated regulatory timeline, anchored to published CDSCO timelines for AI/ML diagnostics, is 18–30 months. The 90-day priorities are: stand up ISO 13485 + IEC 62304 documentation, contract a NABL-accredited reference laboratory to anchor the validation dataset, and request a CDSCO pre-submission meeting to confirm the SaMD pathway, ACP scope, and clinical evaluation expectations. The path is viable but multi-year, and submission maturity should be sequenced behind these foundational dependencies.
executive_summary.product_class: Class C IVD-SaMD (AI-based digital pathology grading software)
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing — MD-12 test licence for clinical performance evaluation, followed by MD-9 manufacturing licence from the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, with ACP documentation aligned to the Oct 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft
headline_gaps: Establish ISO 13485 + IEC 62304 QMS through a structured gap assessment before MD-12 drafting.
Contract a NABL-accredited (ISO 15189) pathology laboratory to anchor the validation dataset and clinical performance evaluation.
Open a SUGAM account and request a CDSCO pre-submission meeting to clarify SaMD pathway and ACP expectations.

intended_use.indication: OncoPath Assist is intended as a diagnostic-aid software for the analysis of digitised histopathology slide images, providing suggested cancer grading to support a qualified pathologist's interpretation. The output is intended to be used as decision support and not as a standalone diagnostic determination. Specific tumour types, organ systems, and grading schemes (for example, Gleason for prostate or Nottingham for breast) are expected to be defined and locked at the time of submission, with each indication validated independently.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are likely qualified anatomic pathologists and laboratory professionals working in NABL-accredited (ISO 15189) pathology laboratories or hospital histopathology departments. The software is not intended for use by patients, general practitioners, or non-specialist clinicians, and outputs are not intended to be released directly to patients without pathologist review.
intended_use.use_environment: The software is expected to be deployed in digital pathology environments within hospital laboratories or reference pathology laboratories, typically integrated with whole-slide imaging (WSI) scanners and laboratory information systems (LIS). Deployment may be on-premise or cloud-hosted; in either case, data handling is expected to comply with the DPDP Act, 2023, and applicable hospital IT controls.
intended_use.contraindications: The software is not intended for primary screening in the absence of pathologist review, for use on slide images acquired outside validated WSI scanner specifications, or for tumour types and staining protocols not included in the validated indications. Use on paediatric specimens, intra-operative frozen sections, or cytology preparations is likely to be out of scope unless specifically validated.

device_description.components_architecture: OncoPath Assist is a software-only medical device (SaMD) comprising: (i) a DICOM/WSI ingestion module that accepts whole-slide images from supported scanners; (ii) a pre-processing pipeline for tile extraction, stain normalisation, and quality control; (iii) a trained deep-learning inference engine that produces region-level and slide-level grading predictions; (iv) a pathologist-facing review interface displaying heatmaps, suggested grades, and confidence indicators; and (v) audit, logging, and model-version-tracking components. The architecture is expected to be documented per IEC 62304 software safety classification, likely Class B or C.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software applies a trained convolutional neural network (or comparable deep-learning architecture) to histopathology image tiles to identify morphological features associated with tumour grade. Tile-level predictions are aggregated using a slide-level model to produce a suggested grade, accompanied by a confidence score and a visual heatmap highlighting regions that drove the prediction. The pathologist retains full diagnostic authority and may accept, modify, or reject the suggestion.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, OncoPath Assist has no patient-contacting materials. Applicable standards likely include IEC 62304 (software lifecycle), ISO 14971 (risk management), ISO 13485 (QMS), IEC 82304-1 (health software), and IEC 62366-1 (usability). For AI-specific aspects, alignment is expected with emerging CDSCO SaMD draft guidance (Oct 2025), ICMR 2023 AI-in-healthcare guidelines, and applicable IMDRF SaMD documents.
device_description.variants_accessories: Initial submission is expected to cover a defined set of indication variants (for example, by tumour type and grading scheme), each validated independently. The software does not include physical accessories. Compatible whole-slide imaging scanners are expected to be listed as validated input devices; staining protocols and slide preparation specifications likely form part of the labelled conditions of use.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Software lifecycle is expected to follow IEC 62304, including planned releases, security patches, and model updates. Model updates that fall within the pre-defined Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) are expected to follow the change protocol; changes outside the ACP envelope likely require a fresh regulatory notification. End-of-life involves controlled de-installation, secure deletion of cached patient data per DPDP requirements, and customer notification per QMS procedures.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Cancer grading directly informs oncology treatment decisions, including surgical planning, adjuvant therapy selection, and prognostic counselling. Under the IMDRF SaMD framework (IMDRF/SaMD WG/N12), the information provided is best characterised as 'drive clinical management' because the suggested grade typically guides the next clinical step rather than merely informing it. The healthcare situation is 'serious' — oncology decisions carry meaningful morbidity and mortality implications, though the pathologist remains the diagnostic decision-maker, which is why the situation is not classified as 'critical'. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' × 'serious' maps to IMDRF Category III, reflecting elevated risk warranting structured clinical evaluation, robust software lifecycle controls, and post-market performance monitoring.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017, IVD software that informs diagnosis or treatment decisions in serious clinical states is typically classified as Class C, regulated through the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority. The Oct 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance reinforces this placement for AI-based diagnostic software acting on serious conditions such as oncology. The IMDRF Category III mapping is consistent with CDSCO Class C: both frameworks recognise the elevated clinical consequence of an incorrect output and the need for clinical performance evaluation, structured QMS evidence, and an Algorithm Change Protocol. Final classification is expected to be confirmed in a CDSCO pre-submission meeting, particularly given the evolving SaMD-specific guidance.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Histopathology grading is a cornerstone of oncology diagnosis but is subject to documented inter-observer variability, particularly for borderline grades. India faces a structural shortage of trained anatomic pathologists relative to cancer case volumes, leading to reporting backlogs in tier-2 and tier-3 cities. AI-assisted grading is positioned to improve consistency, reduce turnaround time, and support pathologists in high-volume settings, while preserving the pathologist as the diagnostic authority.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Internationally, several AI digital pathology tools have received regulatory clearances (for example, FDA-cleared and CE-marked prostate and breast pathology AI products). Indian predicate identification under MDR 2017 is less formalised than the FDA 510(k) framework; however, comparator analysis against internationally cleared digital pathology AI is expected to support the submission narrative. A documented predicate/comparator review is likely to be prepared as part of the technical file, with explicit identification of similarities, differences, and additional validation needed for the Indian context.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence plan is expected to include: (i) a retrospective analytical performance study on a curated, NABL-lab-anchored slide dataset with documented ground truth from multiple expert pathologists; (ii) a prospective clinical performance evaluation under an EC-approved protocol aligned with ICMR 2023 AI-in-healthcare guidance, conducted at NABL-accredited sites; (iii) demographic and scanner-diversity sub-analyses to characterise generalisability; and (iv) a post-market performance monitoring plan tied to the ACP. Sample sizes, endpoints, and acceptance criteria are expected to be confirmed during CDSCO pre-submission engagement.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan is expected to be prepared in alignment with the Oct 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance and informed by FDA PCCP and IMDRF principles. The PCCP is likely to define the envelope of permissible model updates — for example, periodic re-training on expanded datasets covering additional scanner types or demographic strata, with the indication, output type, and intended use held constant. Out-of-envelope changes, such as adding a new tumour type or moving from decision-support to autonomous reporting, are expected to require fresh regulatory notification.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change protocol is expected to specify, for each anticipated change type: data requirements (volume, source, NABL-lab provenance, demographic representation), re-training and validation methodology, performance acceptance thresholds (sensitivity, specificity, agreement with expert consensus), regression testing against locked reference datasets, version control and traceability per IEC 62304, and internal QMS sign-off gates before deployment. The protocol is likely to be versioned and maintained as a controlled document within the ISO 13485 QMS.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market performance monitoring is expected to include: (i) ongoing tracking of pathologist override rates and discordance patterns; (ii) periodic re-evaluation of model performance on prospectively collected slides from deployed sites; (iii) drift detection across scanner types, staining protocols, and patient demographics; (iv) a structured complaint-handling and adverse-event reporting process aligned with CDSCO Materiovigilance expectations; and (v) periodic reports to CDSCO summarising performance, ACP-scope changes deployed, and any out-of-envelope changes triggering fresh notification. The monitoring plan is likely to be referenced in the MD-9 submission and maintained under the QMS.

executive_summary.body:
OncoPath Assist is an artificial intelligence–based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) designed to analyse digitised pathology slide images and suggest likely cancer grade classifications to support pathologist decision-making. Based on its intended diagnostic function in a serious clinical context, the product is expected to be classified as a Class C IVD-SaMD under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR 2017), placing it under the Central Licensing Authority pathway administered by CDSCO.

The IMDRF risk framework maps OncoPath Assist to Category III: the software is intended to drive clinical management of a serious condition (oncology diagnosis and grading), where erroneous output could directly influence treatment planning and patient outcomes. This Category III profile aligns with the Class C designation under MDR 2017 and is consistent with the regulatory expectations articulated in CDSCO's October 2025 SaMD Draft Guidance, which introduces Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) requirements for adaptive AI/ML diagnostics.

Current readiness is assessed at an early foundational stage across all five regulatory dimensions. No Quality Management System aligned to ISO 13485 or IEC 62304 has been documented, no NABL-accredited laboratory partnership for clinical performance evaluation has been identified, and no formal CDSCO engagement has been initiated. These three gaps represent the most material near-term risks to a timely submission.

The anticipated regulatory pathway involves a test license application (MD-12) to enable clinical investigation, a clinical performance evaluation dossier anchored to NABL-accredited ground-truth datasets, and a central manufacturing/import license (MD-9) supported by the full technical dossier. The overall timeline to market authorisation is estimated at 18–30 months from the initiation of foundational activities, subject to CDSCO review cycles and the evolving SaMD guidance framework.

The immediate 90-day priority is to initiate ISO 13485 and IEC 62304 gap assessments, secure a NABL-accredited pathology laboratory partnership, and open a SUGAM account to request a CDSCO pre-submission meeting.
executive_summary.pathway: Central licensing pathway — MD-12 test license application to CDSCO Central Licensing Authority to enable clinical performance evaluation, followed by MD-9 central manufacturing/import license supported by full technical dossier including Algorithm Change Protocol; clinical investigation conducted under ICMR Ethics Committee oversight with NABL-accredited laboratory as ground-truth partner
headline_gaps: Initiate ISO 13485 + IEC 62304 gap assessment immediately — neither QMS framework is documented, and both are baseline expectations for a Class C SaMD dossier.
Contract with at least one NABL-accredited (ISO 15189) pathology laboratory to anchor the clinical performance evaluation dataset before drafting MD-12.
Open a SUGAM account and request a CDSCO pre-submission meeting to confirm the SaMD pathway, ACP format, and clinical evidence expectations before committing resources to the full dossier.

intended_use.indication: OncoPath Assist is intended as a decision-support tool for the computer-aided analysis of digitised histopathology slide images to suggest likely cancer grade classifications. The software processes whole-slide images (WSI) or region-of-interest image tiles submitted by trained pathology professionals and returns a grading suggestion (e.g., low-grade / high-grade, or tumour-type-specific grading scales as applicable) along with a confidence indicator. The output is intended to assist, and not replace, the clinical judgement of a qualified pathologist in establishing a final diagnostic grade.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended primary user is a qualified pathologist or histopathology technician operating within a clinical or diagnostic laboratory setting. Secondary users may include oncologists reviewing digitally reported cases. The software is not intended for use by patients or untrained lay persons. Users are expected to have formal training in histopathology interpretation and to possess the clinical competence necessary to evaluate, accept, or override the system's suggestions in the context of the full clinical picture.
intended_use.use_environment: OncoPath Assist is intended for use within NABL-accredited or equivalent clinical pathology laboratories, hospital-based pathology departments, and digital pathology reporting centres equipped with validated whole-slide imaging infrastructure. The software may be deployed as a web-based or locally hosted application integrated with laboratory information systems (LIS) or digital pathology platforms. It is not intended for use in emergency, point-of-care, or resource-limited settings where immediate treatment decisions are made without pathologist oversight.
intended_use.contraindications: OncoPath Assist is not intended for autonomous diagnostic reporting without pathologist review and sign-off. The software is not validated for cytology specimens, frozen-section intraoperative analysis, or non-oncology histopathological assessments outside its validated indication scope. It is not intended for paediatric oncology grading unless specific clinical validation data support such use. The system is not to be used as the sole basis for initiating, withholding, or modifying cancer therapy. Use on image data derived from scanners or staining protocols outside the validated configuration may yield unreliable outputs.

device_description.components_architecture: OncoPath Assist comprises the following logical components: (1) a secure image ingestion module that accepts whole-slide images (WSI) in standard formats such as SVS, NDPI, or TIFF via API or direct upload; (2) a deep-learning inference engine hosting the trained convolutional neural network (CNN) or transformer-based model responsible for feature extraction and grade prediction; (3) a post-processing and confidence-scoring module that translates raw model outputs into clinically labelled grading suggestions with associated probability scores; (4) a user-facing reporting interface presenting results to the pathologist alongside relevant slide regions highlighted as evidence; and (5) an audit and logging module recording all inference events, user actions, and model version identifiers for traceability. The system is expected to be deployed as a SaaS platform or on-premise server installation, with role-based access controls.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software applies supervised deep-learning techniques — likely convolutional neural networks or vision transformer architectures — trained on annotated whole-slide image datasets labelled by expert pathologists. During inference, the system tiles the input slide into fixed-size image patches, extracts morphological and textural features at multiple magnification levels, aggregates patch-level predictions (commonly via attention-based multiple-instance learning), and produces a slide-level cancer grade suggestion. The model's output probabilities are mapped to a defined grading schema (e.g., Gleason, WHO/ISUP, or tumour-specific scales). The system does not modify the underlying slide data and operates in a read-only, assistive capacity.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, OncoPath Assist has no physical materials. Applicable standards are expected to include: IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle), ISO 13485 (quality management system), ISO 14971 (risk management), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), ISO/IEC 27001 (information security management), and ISO 15223-1 (symbols). For IVD-specific performance evaluation, ISO 18113 series and applicable CLSI EP guidelines may be referenced. Compliance with DICOM and HL7 FHIR interoperability standards is anticipated where LIS integration is implemented. Adherence to ICMR's Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare (2023) is expected for clinical study design.
device_description.variants_accessories: At initial submission, OncoPath Assist is anticipated to be presented as a single software variant scoped to a defined cancer type and grading schema (to be specified in the final dossier). Future variants addressing additional cancer types or grading scales would likely require separate regulatory submissions or substantial equivalence justification. The software is not supplied with proprietary hardware accessories; it is designed to operate with commercially available, validated whole-slide image scanners from third-party manufacturers. Validated scanner models and staining protocols are to be listed in the Instructions for Use (IFU) as part of the intended configuration.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: The software lifecycle is governed by a documented change management procedure aligned with IEC 62304 software maintenance requirements. Version control is maintained through a configuration management system with each released version assigned a unique identifier recorded in the audit log. Planned algorithm updates — including retraining, fine-tuning, or architecture changes — are subject to the Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) and assessed against predefined performance thresholds before deployment. End-of-life decommissioning involves secure deletion of customer data per DPDP Act obligations and ISO/IEC 27001-aligned data retention policies. No physical disposal considerations apply to the software itself.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorisation Framework (N12 FINAL:2014), risk category is determined by the intersection of the significance of information provided by the SaMD and the healthcare situation or condition it addresses. OncoPath Assist provides cancer grade suggestions that are directly used by pathologists to inform oncology diagnosis and, subsequently, treatment planning — placing it in the 'Drive clinical management' significance tier. The healthcare situation is 'Serious': cancer grading errors carry a meaningful risk of incorrect or delayed treatment, which could result in significant patient harm. The intersection of 'Drive clinical management' × 'Serious' maps to IMDRF Category III. This categorisation is consistent with analogous AI-based cancer diagnostic tools reviewed under comparable international frameworks and with the risk profile articulated in CDSCO's October 2025 SaMD Draft Guidance.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (Third Schedule), Class C encompasses medical devices and IVDs that pose moderate-to-high risk, including active devices and IVDs used for the detection or staging of serious or life-threatening conditions. OncoPath Assist, as an AI-based IVD-SaMD that provides cancer grading information to drive pathologist-led clinical management decisions, aligns with the Class C profile. The IMDRF Category III determination further supports this classification. Class C devices are subject to the Central Licensing Authority pathway, requiring a test license (MD-12) for clinical investigation and a central manufacturing or import license (MD-9) supported by a full technical and clinical performance evaluation dossier. The AI/ML adaptive component additionally triggers Algorithm Change Protocol obligations under the evolving CDSCO SaMD framework.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Accurate histopathological cancer grading is a foundational determinant of oncology treatment selection, prognosis estimation, and clinical trial eligibility. In India, the cancer burden is substantial — with over 1.4 million new cases estimated annually — yet access to specialised pathology expertise is geographically uneven, with a significant shortage of qualified histopathologists outside Tier 1 cities. Inter-observer variability in manual grading (documented in international literature across prostate, breast, and colorectal cancers, among others) represents a recognised source of diagnostic inconsistency. OncoPath Assist is intended to address this need by providing a reproducible, AI-assisted grading suggestion that supports pathologist decision-making, potentially reducing variability and enabling faster turnaround in high-volume or resource-constrained laboratory settings.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Several AI-based digital pathology tools have received regulatory authorisation in comparable jurisdictions and may serve as reference predicates for technical and clinical benchmarking. These include Paige Prostate (FDA De Novo authorised, 2021) for prostate cancer detection, Ibex Galen Prostate (CE-marked) for prostate cancer grading, and Aiforia Create-based oncology models deployed in European clinical settings. In the Indian context, no directly analogous Class C IVD-SaMD for cancer grading has been identified in the public CDSCO device registry at the time of this dossier preparation; the CDSCO pre-submission meeting is expected to clarify whether a predicate-based pathway or a de novo pathway applies under the evolving SaMD framework.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy for OncoPath Assist is anticipated to comprise three phases. First, a retrospective analytical validation study using archived, de-identified whole-slide images from a NABL-accredited (ISO 15189) pathology laboratory, establishing sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and AUC against expert pathologist ground-truth labels. Second, a prospective reader study comparing pathologist grading accuracy and concordance with and without AI assistance, conducted under an ICMR Ethics Committee–approved protocol. Third, a real-world performance monitoring plan post-commercialisation, feeding into the ACP-governed model update cycle. Sample size calculations, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and statistical analysis plans are to be finalised in consultation with a biostatistician and documented in a Clinical Performance Evaluation Plan (CPEP) prior to MD-12 submission.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is to be established prior to MD-12 submission, documenting the categories of permissible algorithm changes — including retraining on expanded datasets, hyperparameter adjustments, and architectural modifications — that may be implemented without triggering a full re-submission, provided predefined performance thresholds are maintained. The PCCP is expected to specify the scope of each change type, the analytical validation required to qualify the change, the approval authority within the QMS, and the notification obligation to CDSCO. The PCCP will be maintained as a controlled document under the ISO 13485 QMS and referenced in the technical dossier submitted with the MD-9 application.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) will define a structured governance process for all planned and unplanned changes to the OncoPath Assist inference model. For each change category, the protocol will specify: (1) a performance benchmark set (locked reference dataset held out from training) against which the updated model is evaluated; (2) minimum acceptable performance thresholds for sensitivity, specificity, and AUC relative to the validated baseline; (3) a risk assessment per ISO 14971 for the proposed change; (4) a user notification and version-labelling procedure; and (5) a regulatory notification matrix distinguishing changes that may be implemented under the PCCP from those requiring a new or supplementary CDSCO submission. The ACP will be aligned with CDSCO's October 2025 SaMD Draft Guidance expectations as they are finalised.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: A post-market real-world performance monitoring programme will be established as part of the post-market surveillance (PMS) plan required under MDR 2017. The programme is anticipated to include: periodic sampling of de-identified inference outputs for pathologist concordance review; tracking of complaint and near-miss events attributable to model output; monitoring of input data drift (e.g., changes in scanner models, staining reagents, or tissue fixation protocols used by customers); and an annual performance summary report reviewed by the Medical Safety Officer and QMS management representative. Findings from real-world monitoring will feed into the ACP-governed retraining and update cycle, and material performance deviations will trigger the CDSCO notification procedure defined in the ACP.
